sources.notes: Read the full statement, examples, and constraints.
Confirms the forced min-max equality, extremal rectangle, two-set inclusion-exclusion, and binomial-theorem collapse.
Actually read; independently derives the equality characterization and row-set binomial inversion.
Actually read; gives the alternative threshold-event F(x,y) inclusion-exclusion formulation.

annotation.summary: Count n by m matrices over [1,v] whose minimum row maximum is at most their maximum column minimum.
annotation.constraints: 1 <= t <= 8000; 1 <= n,v and n*v <= 10^6; 1 <= m <= 10^9; the sum of n*v over tests is at most 10^6; answers are modulo 998244353. Iterating columns, cells, or matrices is impossible.
annotation.core_idea: Let A=min_i max_j a[i][j] and B=max_j min_i a[i][j]. If row r realizes A and column c realizes B, then A >= a[r][c] >= B, so every matrix has B <= A and the requested inequality holds exactly when A=B=k. For fixed k, use inclusion-exclusion over a nonempty chosen row set R whose entries are constrained to be <=k and a nonempty chosen column set C whose entries are constrained to be >=k. Every cell in R x C is then forced to k, so those rows have maximum k and those columns have minimum k. For |R|=i and |C|=j the block count is k^(i(m-j))*(v-k+1)^((n-i)j)*v^((n-i)(m-j)), with coefficient (-1)^(i+j) C(n,i) C(m,j). Put X=k^i*v^(n-i) and Y=(v-k+1)^(n-i). The entire nonempty-column sum is sum_{j=1}^m (-1)^j C(m,j) X^(m-j)Y^j=(X-Y)^m-X^m. Therefore the answer is sum_{k=1}^v sum_{i=1}^n (-1)^i C(n,i) ((X-Y)^m-X^m), evaluated modulo 998244353. Precompute combinations and maintain the powers with exponent at most n while binary exponentiation handles m.
annotation.complexity: O(n*v*log m) time per test after O(n) combinatorial preprocessing, and O(n) auxiliary memory; the aggregate n*v over all tests is at most 10^6.
tricks: use the universal max-min <= min-max inequality to force equality
the extremal cells form a Cartesian product of nonempty row and column sets
apply inclusion-exclusion over both extremal row and column events
collapse the whole column-subset-size sum with the binomial theorem

solution_variants.name: extremal-rectangle-binomial-collapse
solution_variants.summary: Enumerate the common extremal value k and inclusion-exclude nonempty sets of rows bounded above by k and columns bounded below by k. Count the resulting four matrix blocks, then use the binomial theorem to eliminate the column-set-size loop and retain only k and the row-set size.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n*v*log m) time and O(n) memory

solution_variants.name: threshold-discrete-difference
solution_variants.summary: Define F(x,y) as the number of matrices in which every row has maximum at least x and every column has minimum at most y. Inclusion-excluding i rows that violate the first condition gives F(x,y)=sum_i (-1)^i C(n,i) [v^(n-i)(x-1)^i-(v-y)^(n-i)max(x-y-1,0)^i]^m. The matrices with A=B=k are F(k,k)-F(k+1,k)-F(k,k-1)+F(k+1,k-1); sum this over k.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n*v*log m) time with maintained power tables (or O(n*v*(log n+log m)) with direct exponentiation) and O(n) memory

tags.evidence: Validity is equality of two extrema, and for each k the main variant inclusion-excludes nonempty row-max and column-min event sets whose intersection is forced to k.
The binomial theorem turns the sum over all j=1..m chosen-column counts into (X-Y)^m-X^m, removing an impossible O(m) factor when m can be 10^9.
The alternate variant inclusion-excludes rows violating max>=x and uses a four-term discrete difference to force both extrema to equal k.
The solution counts choices of extremal row and column sets with binomial coefficients and independent block fillings.
The structural reduction starts from the universal inequality max_j min_i a[i][j] <= min_i max_j a[i][j], so the requested reverse inequality forces equality.
Factorials, inverse factorials, signs, and large-exponent powers are evaluated modulo 998244353.
